did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an improved EfficientNetB0 deep learning model, incorporating Ghost modules and CBAM attention mechanisms, to perform skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an 'improved EfficientNetB0 network' for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods (Ghost module, CBAM attention), datasets (HAM10000), experimental setups (transfer learning, hyperparameters), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy 89.38%, 5.19M parameters) compared to baseline models. Although published in a conference proceedings (NTCI 2024), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Study on Skin Lesions Classification Based on Improved EfficientNetB0 Network
journal: 2024 International Conference on New Trends in Computational Intelligence (NTCI 2024)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification by proposing an improved EfficientNetB0 network that integrates Ghost modules and CBAM attention mechanisms to reduce computational complexity. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset from the ISIC2018 challenge, utilizing transfer learning from ImageNet weights to handle class imbalance and limited labeled samples. Experimental results demonstrated that the improved model achieved an accuracy of 89.38%, with Macro Precision, Recall, and F1-Score of 0.89, 0.86, and 0.87, respectively. The proposed architecture significantly reduced the parameter count to 5.19M compared to baseline models like DenseNet201 and Xception, while maintaining high performance. Ablation studies confirmed that the combination of Ghost and CBAM modules yielded better accuracy than the original EfficientNetB0 or versions with only one of these modules. These results indicate the model is suitable for deployment on mobile devices to assist in clinical skin disease diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, DF, BKL, VASC, NV, MEL)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs malignant or specific lesion types) using categorical crossentropy loss and reports accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: The HAM10000 skin disease dataset from the ISIC2018 challenge, containing 10,015 dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the improved EfficientNetB0 model after data augmentation (normalization, rotation, shifts) and balancing class weights.
use.validation: Used for validation during training (1803 images) to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score (1002 images).
use.other: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet were used for transfer learning, but ImageNet is not the primary dataset for the skin lesion task.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Confusion matrix metrics (TP, FP, FN, TN)

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and parameter counts without statistical significance analysis.

HAM10000.notes: Compared with the EfficientNetB0 without CBAM, the accuracy of the EfficientNetB0 with CBAM is 88.64%, and the accuracy of the improved EfficientNetB0 network with both CBAM and Ghost modules is 89.38%, which is significantly higher than the unimproved EfficientNetB0. The loss function value of the improved EfficientNetB0 network is the lowest at 0.5272.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from ImageNet dataset weights (over 10 million images)

modifications: Replaced the first stage convolutional layer (Conv3x3) with a Ghost module
Added CBAM (Convolutional Block Attention Module) between the 9th stage convolutional layer and the pooling layer

components.ghost_module: Replaces standard convolution in the first stage to generate ghost feature maps using deep convolution and linear transformations, reducing computational resources and parameters.
components.cbam_module: Consists of Channel Attention Module (CAM) and Spatial Attention Module (SAM). Placed after the 9th stage convolutional layer and before the pooling layer to enhance important feature layers and suppress ineffective ones.

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Category weight method (approx. 1400 images per category after preprocessing)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected (fc) layer following Global Average Pooling

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing: Original images (600x450) uniformly resized to 224x224
Horizontal shifts: Applied via Keras ImageDataGenerator
Vertical shifts: Applied via Keras ImageDataGenerator
Class weight balancing: Category weight method used to handle sample imbalance, resulting in approximately 1400 images per category

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training and validation sets using Keras ImageDataGenerator.

conclusions: The proposed improved EfficientNetB0 network, which integrates Ghost modules and CBAM attention mechanisms, achieved a classification accuracy of 89.38% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The improved model demonstrated superior performance compared to the baseline EfficientNetB0 (85.07% accuracy) and versions with only one of the added modules (EfficientNetB0 with CBAM achieved 88.64% accuracy).
The improved EfficientNetB0 model significantly reduced computational complexity, requiring only 5.19 million parameters compared to baseline models like DenseNet201 (19.3M) and Xception (21.9M).
The model achieved a single inference time of 71ms, indicating its suitability for deployment on mobile devices with limited computing power.
The improved model outperformed other referenced models on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving higher accuracy than ResNet50 (83.26%), AlexNet variants (83%), and MobileNetV2 (82.35%).

The combination of Ghost modules and CBAM attention mechanisms yielded better accuracy and lower loss function values (0.5272) than the original EfficientNetB0 or versions with only one of these modules.

The study acknowledges that existing skin disease classification networks often suffer from large parameter counts and complex operations, which hinder mobile deployment, a limitation addressed by this lightweight architecture.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset from the ISIC2018 challenge, which may introduce population bias as the dataset is not globally representative of all skin types and demographics.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test split of the HAM10000 dataset, limiting generalizability to other clinical settings or datasets.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons between the proposed model and baselines, relying solely on absolute metric differences.
Class imbalance was addressed using category weights rather than resampling techniques, which may mask performance issues on rare classes like VASC (vascular malformation) and AKIEC.

The reported accuracy of 89.38% in the abstract and text conflicts with Table III which lists 90.38% for the Improved EfficientNetB0, though Table V (Ablation Study) confirms 89.38%; this inconsistency requires clarification.

The model was trained on a high-end GPU (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090), which may not reflect the constraints of the target deployment environment (mobile devices) where inference time and memory are critical.

Data leakage risks are present if the HAM10000 dataset's known patient overlap across splits was not strictly managed, although the paper claims a 9:1 then 8:2 split which is standard but not explicitly verified for patient-level separation.

The comparison with other references (e.g., Reference [18], [19]) uses metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1) that may not be directly comparable if those references used different evaluation protocols or subsets of the HAM10000 dataset.
